terms.def: Ask price − bid price; the immediate cost of trading; compensates market makers for inventory + adverse selection risk.
The price move caused by executing a trade; large orders push prices against the trader.
The process by which trading reveals new information and incorporates it into prices.
Risk that the counterparty has superior information; market makers widen spreads to compensate for this risk.
Minimum price increment for quoting; smaller tick sizes narrow spreads but can reduce market depth.
Limit Order Book — the queue of all resting limit orders; shows the supply and demand at every price level.

eli3: Market microstructure is the study of how trades actually happen — the mechanics of buying and selling securities. It covers: how prices are set at any given moment, how buyers and sellers find each other, how quickly trades execute, and how trading costs (beyond the obvious commission) affect investors.

The key insight from microstructure research: the price you see quoted and the price you actually pay are often different. The 'bid-ask spread' is the gap between what a buyer pays and what a seller receives — it's the market maker's compensation for providing liquidity. Understanding microstructure helps explain patterns in intraday price data and is essential for algorithmic trading and execution.

Market microstructure became central to finance after electronic trading replaced floor trading. HFT (high-frequency trading) firms operate entirely at the microstructure level — profiting from tiny price differences and information asymmetries measured in microseconds.

whenWhy.title: Understanding price discovery and information incorporation
whenWhy.body: Prices in financial markets aggregate dispersed information from millions of participants. Microstructure explains how this happens: informed traders (who have superior information) trade against market makers; market makers observe order flow and update prices to reflect the information revealed. The Kyle (1985) model formalised this: a strategic informed trader gradually incorporates private information into prices; the speed of price discovery depends on market maker sophistication and trading volume. This framework underpins modern understanding of market efficiency and has practical implications for HFT strategies.

whenWhy.title: Transaction cost analysis for institutional execution
whenWhy.body: For institutional investors managing large positions, transaction costs are a major performance drag. Microstructure decomposes transaction costs into: (1) explicit costs — commissions and fees; (2) bid-ask spread — paid to the market maker; (3) market impact — price moves against you as you execute a large order; (4) opportunity cost — the cost of not trading when prices moved favorably before execution. Implementation Shortfall (IS) measures the total cost relative to the decision price. Large orders must be sliced (TWAP, VWAP algorithms) to minimise market impact. Understanding microstructure is essential for optimal execution.

whenWhy.title: Regulation of fair and orderly markets
whenWhy.body: Market microstructure research drives financial regulation. Decimalization (2001, US): switching from 1/8 fractional to penny pricing tightened bid-ask spreads, benefiting retail investors but reducing market maker profits. RegNMS (2005, US): required brokers to route orders to the venue with the best displayed price (National Best Bid and Offer, NBBO). MiFID II (2018, Europe): transparency requirements for pre- and post-trade reporting. Flash crashes (2010, 2015): microstructure feedback loops — HFT liquidity withdrawal amplified price moves. These regulatory events are all rooted in microstructure phenomena.

deepDiveIntro: Market microstructure theory has two pillars: (1) Inventory models — market makers set prices to manage their inventory; they widen spreads when their inventory is imbalanced (too long or too short) to attract offsetting order flow (Stoll 1978, Ho & Stoll 1981); (2) Information asymmetry models — market makers face adverse selection from informed traders; they widen spreads to break even against traders who know more than they do (Glosten & Milgrom 1985, Kyle 1985). In reality, both effects operate simultaneously.

Modern electronic markets use continuous limit order books (LOBs): buyers post bids at various price levels; sellers post asks; trades occur when orders cross. The LOB is the central mechanism for most equity, futures, and FX markets. HFT firms participate as market makers (posting limit orders) and as directional traders (exploiting short-term price patterns in order flow). The average holding period for HFT strategies is seconds to minutes; they profit from tiny per-trade margins at enormous volume.

deepDive.heading: Bid-Ask Spread: Components and Drivers
deepDive.body: The bid-ask spread has three components: (1) Order processing costs — fixed costs of running a market-making operation (technology, staff, capital); decline with scale; (2) Inventory risk premium — market makers hold unwanted inventory when they can't immediately find the other side of a trade; they demand compensation for the price risk of holding this inventory; (3) Adverse selection cost — the expected loss from trading against informed counterparties who know the 'true' value. The adverse selection component dominates for large trades (institutional orders have more information content than retail). Empirically: spreads are wider for small-cap stocks (low volume → less competition among market makers, higher inventory risk), at market open/close (higher information asymmetry), and during earnings announcements (high adverse selection risk). [1]

deepDive.heading: Market Impact and Implementation Shortfall
deepDive.body: Market impact: the adverse price movement caused by your own trading. A buy order of 100,000 shares (large relative to daily volume) pushes the price up — you pay more for later tranches than earlier ones. Market impact decomposes into: (1) temporary impact — price recovers after your order is complete; (2) permanent impact — price doesn't fully recover, reflecting the information content of your trade. Implementation Shortfall (Perold 1988): IS = (actual execution price − decision price) / decision price. IS captures: explicit costs + spread + market impact + opportunity cost. Optimal execution algorithms (Almgren-Chriss 2001): minimise a weighted combination of expected IS and IS variance; a risk-averse trader executes faster (less opportunity cost, more market impact); a risk-neutral trader executes slowly (minimises market impact at the cost of higher opportunity cost variance). [2]

deepDive.heading: High-Frequency Trading and Modern Market Making
deepDive.body: HFT firms provide the majority of market-making in modern equity markets (estimated 50%+ of US equity volume). HFT market makers: post limit orders at the best bid/ask (adding liquidity), earn the spread, and cancel/requote when market conditions change (sub-millisecond). They manage adverse selection risk through speed: they can cancel orders before informed traders transact, using signals from correlated markets (futures, ETFs) to detect when order flow is information-driven. HFT market making improves market quality (tighter spreads, deeper LOB) but raises fairness concerns: co-location (proximity to exchange matching engines) provides unfair speed advantage. The empirical consensus: HFT reduces spreads and increases liquidity in normal conditions but can withdraw liquidity in stress conditions (Flash Crash 2010). [1]

takeaways: Bid-ask spread components: order processing costs + inventory risk + adverse selection — adverse selection dominates for large trades. [1]
Implementation Shortfall: total execution cost vs. decision price = spread + market impact + opportunity cost. [2]
Almgren-Chriss optimal execution: balance market impact (slow) vs. opportunity cost variance (fast). [2]
HFT market makers provide ~50%+ of US equity volume; improve spreads but may withdraw in stress. [1]
LOB (limit order book): the central mechanism — bids and asks cross to form trades at the National Best Bid and Offer. [1]

edgeCases.q: What causes a flash crash and how does microstructure explain it?
edgeCases.a: The 2010 Flash Crash (May 6): the Dow Jones fell 9% in minutes, then recovered almost entirely within 20 minutes. Microstructure explanation: (1) A large sell order (~$4.1B of E-mini S&P futures) entered the market via an algorithm that ignored price and time constraints; (2) HFT market makers absorbed the selling but quickly accumulated large inventories; (3) HFT firms began 'hot potato' trading — passing the unwanted inventory to each other, adding volume without absorbing the sell pressure; (4) HFT market makers withdrew from the market when their risk limits were hit; (5) Bid-ask spreads widened dramatically; some stocks traded at $0.01 or $100,000 on stub quotes; (6) With no market makers, the market was frozen. Recovery: trading halts triggered circuit breakers; normal market makers returned and prices recovered. Lessons: over-reliance on algorithmic market makers that can withdraw simultaneously creates systemic fragility; circuit breakers are now a standard safeguard.

edgeCases.q: How does the limit order book affect price discovery differently from a quote-driven market?
edgeCases.a: Two market structures: (1) Order-driven (limit order book): participants anonymously post orders; price discovery emerges from the interaction of submitted orders; no designated market maker required. Price discovery quality depends on the depth and spread of the LOB. Modern equity exchanges (NYSE, NASDAQ, LSE) use LOBs with designated market makers playing a smaller role. (2) Quote-driven (dealer market): designated dealers continuously post bid and ask quotes; all trades occur at dealer quotes. Traditional OTC bond and FX markets use this structure. Differences: LOBs provide price transparency (you see the full order book) and tighter spreads for liquid instruments; dealer markets provide assured liquidity (dealers are obligated to quote) but with wider spreads as dealer compensation. Modern markets are hybrid: exchanges use LOBs but include designated market makers (DMMs) for thinly-traded securities to ensure minimum liquidity.

sources.label: Glosten, L. & Milgrom, P. (1985) — Bid, Ask and Transaction Prices in a Specialist Market with Heterogeneously Informed Traders, Journal of Financial Economics
Almgren, R. & Chriss, N. (2001) — Optimal Execution of Portfolio Transactions, Journal of Risk
